articles.title: WebinarTV Secretly Scraped Private Zoom Recovery Meetings
articles.summary: WebinarTV scraped and publicly shared recordings of anonymous 12-step recovery meetings and other private support groups that were held over Zoom, without participants knowing. These are spaces where people talk openly about addiction, trauma, and mental health under an expectation of total privacy and anonymity. The breach is a serious violation of trust for some of the most vulnerable people online.
articles.content:
This one is genuinely alarming. WebinarTV, a platform that records and shares webinar content, secretly scraped Zoom meetings from anonymous 12-step recovery programs and other private support groups. People attending these meetings had no idea they were being recorded and redistributed. These are spaces built entirely on the promise that what you say stays in the room.

For anyone unfamiliar, 12-step programs like Alcoholics Anonymous and Narcotics Anonymous are peer-led recovery groups where people share personal stories about addiction and recovery. The anonymity is not just a preference. It is a core part of how these programs work. Participants often share things they would never say publicly, because they believe the meeting is a safe and private space. WebinarTV shattered that assumption.

What makes this worse is the word secretly. WebinarTV did not tell participants, did not ask for consent, and did not give people a way to opt out. The scraping happened on Zoom, which many support groups moved to during the pandemic and have continued using because it makes meetings accessible to people who cannot travel or leave home. Using Zoom was supposed to make recovery more accessible. Instead it created a vulnerability that WebinarTV exploited.

The privacy implications here go well beyond embarrassment. People in recovery can face real consequences if their participation in addiction treatment becomes public, including job loss, custody issues, and social stigma. WebinarTV taking that choice away from them without consent is a serious harm, not just a technical violation.

This story is a loud reminder that when you join a Zoom call, the security of that space depends entirely on who is running it and who might be watching from the outside. Support groups, therapists, and anyone running sensitive online meetings need to urgently review their Zoom settings, including who can record, who can join via link, and whether waiting rooms and passwords are enabled. WebinarTV's actions were wrong, but the gap in protection they exploited is real and it is not going away on its own.
keyPoints: WebinarTV scraped and shared recordings of private anonymous 12-step recovery meetings held on Zoom
Participants had no knowledge they were being recorded or redistributed
12-step programs rely on strict anonymity and these recordings could cause real harm to people in recovery
This highlights a major vulnerability for any sensitive support group using Zoom or similar platforms
People in recovery risk job loss, custody disputes, and social stigma if their participation becomes public

articles.title: Jeremy Strong Is Mark Zuckerberg in Aaron Sorkin's Social Network Sequel
articles.summary: Jeremy Strong has been cast as Mark Zuckerberg in The Social Reckoning, Aaron Sorkin's follow-up to The Social Network, opening in cinemas this October via Sony. Sorkin is also taking the director's chair this time, replacing David Fincher who helmed the 2010 original. The new film picks up the Facebook story through the era of public backlash, data scandals, and political reckoning.
articles.content:
Jeremy Strong is about to become Mark Zuckerberg. The Social Reckoning, Aaron Sorkin's pseudo-sequel to the 2010 film The Social Network, has dropped its first tease and Strong is front and center as the Facebook founder. Sony is releasing the film this October, and already this feels like one of the most anticipated movies of the year.

A little backstory for anyone who needs it. The Social Network told the story of how Mark Zuckerberg built Facebook at Harvard and the messy legal battles and broken friendships that followed. It was directed by David Fincher and written by Sorkin, and it won three Academy Awards including Best Adapted Screenplay. Jesse Eisenberg played Zuckerberg in that film. Now Sorkin is back with a new story but this time he is stepping into the director's seat himself, with Jeremy Strong taking over the central role.

The shift from Fincher to Sorkin as director is a fascinating one. Fincher brought a cold, clinical precision to The Social Network that gave it a thriller-like tension. Sorkin as a director, best known for Molly's Game and Being the Ricardos, tends to go warmer and more dialogue-forward. The Social Reckoning is set against the backdrop of Facebook's years of public backlash including data privacy scandals, Congressional hearings, and accusations of spreading misinformation. That is rich material for a writer who thrives on fast-talking characters in rooms full of moral compromise.

Jeremy Strong is arguably the most exciting dramatic actor working right now. His run on Succession, where he played Kendall Roy and won an Emmy for the role, showed he can carry enormous emotional weight while keeping audiences riveted. Playing a real, living, very powerful person like Mark Zuckerberg is a completely different challenge. Strong will need to find the humanity and the menace in someone the public thinks they already know.

The timing of this release is pointed. Facebook has spent the better part of a decade trying to rebrand its image, renaming its parent company Meta in 2021. A major Sony film arriving this October that drills back into the most uncomfortable chapters of that story is going to spark a serious cultural conversation. Whether Zuckerberg or Meta respond publicly remains to be seen. But The Social Reckoning is already shaping up to be the film everyone is talking about before they have even seen a full trailer.
keyPoints: Jeremy Strong plays Mark Zuckerberg in The Social Reckoning, Aaron Sorkin's follow-up to The Social Network
Aaron Sorkin is directing this time, taking over from David Fincher who directed the 2010 original
Sony releases The Social Reckoning in cinemas this October
The film focuses on the Facebook backlash era including data scandals and political hearings
Jeremy Strong won an Emmy for Succession and is considered one of the best dramatic actors working today

articles.title: TikTok's Satanic E-Girls Brought Acid Bath Back
articles.summary: Louisiana psychedelic sludge metal band Acid Bath went viral on TikTok thanks to a wave of young fans nicknamed the satanic e-girls, and now the band is preparing to play stadiums alongside System of a Down. Singer Dax Riggs called the comeback a mind-blower, and it is easy to see why given that Acid Bath broke up tragically in the late 1990s. This is one of the most unexpected second acts in rock history.
articles.content:
Acid Bath is a band that most people assumed was gone for good. The Louisiana group formed in the early 1990s and built a ferociously loyal cult following with their murky, swamp-soaked blend of sludge metal and psychedelia. Then in 1997 tragedy struck when bassist Audie Pitre was killed in a car accident caused by a drunk driver, and Acid Bath fell apart. For almost three decades, they existed only in the memories of devoted fans and in dusty record collections.

Then TikTok happened. A community of young listeners, mostly women and non-binary fans with a darkly aesthetic online presence, started posting about Acid Bath. The internet quickly dubbed them the satanic e-girls of TikTok. Their posts spread fast and introduced a whole new generation to claggy guitar riffs and circle pit energy that was born years before most of them were alive. Singer Dax Riggs told The Guardian it is a mind-blower, and that feels like an understatement.

What makes this story so fascinating is who is doing the reviving. The stereotype of heavy metal fandom has always leaned toward a certain kind of listener, and these TikTok fans blew that stereotype apart completely. Young women finding themselves in the grinding, swamp-drenched sound of Acid Bath is genuinely a cultural moment worth paying attention to. It says something real about how people use heavy music to process darkness, and it says something about TikTok's power to reach across time and genre in ways that no algorithm was specifically designed to do.

Acid Bath is now set to play stadiums with System of a Down, one of the biggest rock acts still drawing massive live audiences. System of a Down, the Armenian-American metal band known for albums like Toxicity, have a long history of championing underground and cult rock acts. The pairing makes total sense. Both bands carry a heavy emotional weight and an unapologetic weirdness that their fans absolutely love.

For Dax Riggs and the rest of Acid Bath, this is not just a nostalgia tour. It is a genuine reclamation of a legacy that was cut short. The shrooms, alligators and swamp imagery that defined their aesthetic feels weirdly at home in 2026, where dark cottagecore and occult visuals are everywhere online. Acid Bath did not change to find a new audience. The audience caught up to them.

If you have never heard Acid Bath before, now is genuinely the best time to start. Put on When the Kite String Pops and let it hit you. Then go watch the TikTok community that brought them back and feel good about the internet for once.
keyPoints: Acid Bath broke up in 1997 after the death of bassist Audie Pitre in a drunk driving accident
A wave of young TikTok fans called the satanic e-girls went viral posting about the band
Singer Dax Riggs described the comeback as a mind-blower
Acid Bath will now play stadiums with System of a Down
The story shows how TikTok can resurrect cult artists decades after their peak

articles.title: Lena Dunham's Memoir Details Shocking Adam Driver Behaviour
articles.summary: Lena Dunham's new memoir Famesick reveals that Adam Driver was 'verbally aggressive' on the set of Girls, physically hurled her during their first sex scene, and threw a chair at the wall next to her during a rehearsal. Girls ran on HBO from 2012 to 2017 and made both Dunham and Driver into major stars. The book puts a very different light on what many considered a golden era of prestige TV.
articles.content:
Lena Dunham has a lot to say in her new memoir Famesick, and not all of it is comfortable reading. Dunham writes that her Girls co-star Adam Driver was 'verbally aggressive' on set, that he physically hurled her during the filming of their first sex scene together, and that during one rehearsal he threw a chair at the wall right next to her. These are serious allegations about one of Hollywood's biggest stars.

Girls, which aired on HBO from 2012 to 2017, was a defining show of that era. Dunham created, wrote and starred in it as Hannah Horvath, a twentysomething trying to make it in New York. Adam Driver played Adam Sackler, Hannah's complicated on-again off-again boyfriend. Driver's performance on Girls was what first put him on Hollywood's radar before he went on to Star Wars, Marriage Story and many other major films. The two characters had an intense, often chaotic relationship on screen, and it seems some of that energy bled into real life.

Dunham's account raises questions about how much was known or tolerated behind the scenes at the time. The memoir does not appear to frame Driver's behaviour as something that was reported or addressed during production. That silence, if true, fits a pattern that the entertainment industry has been reckoning with for years now. Famesick seems designed to confront some of that head on.

This is a significant cultural moment because it involves two people who are both still very much active and respected in the industry. Adam Driver has not publicly responded to Dunham's claims as of this writing. Famesick is already drawing huge attention and is likely to spark a much wider conversation about on-set conduct and the power dynamics that shaped shows like Girls.
keyPoints: Lena Dunham's memoir Famesick claims Adam Driver was verbally aggressive on the Girls set
Dunham writes that Driver physically hurled her during their first filmed sex scene
Driver allegedly threw a chair at the wall next to Dunham during a rehearsal
Girls ran on HBO from 2012 to 2017 and was the show that launched Driver's career
Adam Driver had not publicly responded to the claims at the time of publication
articles.wikiSearchQuery: Girls HBO TV series Lena Dunham

articles.title: Phil Collins, Oasis and Wu-Tang Clan Enter Rock Hall
articles.summary: Phil Collins, Oasis, Sade, Wu-Tang Clan, Iron Maiden, Billy Idol, Queen Latifah, Joy Division/New Order and the late Luther Vandross have all been named as 2026 Rock and Roll Hall of Fame inductees. This is a massive class that spans decades and genres, from 80s pop and hip-hop to heavy metal and soul. The induction of Oasis is particularly timely given the band's ongoing reunion tour.
articles.content:
The Rock and Roll Hall of Fame just announced its 2026 inductee class and it is genuinely one of the most exciting lineups in years. Phil Collins, Oasis, Sade, Wu-Tang Clan, Iron Maiden, Billy Idol, Queen Latifah, Joy Division/New Order and the late Luther Vandross are all heading in. The Rock and Roll Hall of Fame, based in Cleveland, Ohio, honours artists who have had a lasting impact on rock and its related genres. Acts become eligible 25 years after their first commercial recording.

Oasis getting in feels like perfect timing. Liam and Noel Gallagher famously split the band in 2009 after a backstage fight, and their reunion tour announcement in 2024 sent the internet into meltdown. Now they get to walk into the Hall of Fame together too. That is quite the comeback story.

Wu-Tang Clan's induction is a huge moment for hip-hop. The New York collective, formed in 1992, essentially built a blueprint for how rap groups could operate as a brand, a movement and a business all at once. Their debut album Enter the Wu-Tang (36 Chambers) is still considered one of the greatest records ever made. Seeing them stand alongside Iron Maiden and Sade in the same class says everything about how broad rock and roll culture has always been.

The posthumous recognition of Luther Vandross adds real emotional weight to the ceremony. Vandross, who died in 2005, was one of the greatest soul voices of his generation. Queen Latifah's induction is also a landmark moment. She is one of the few artists who has built a Hall of Fame level career in both music and acting. This year's ceremony is going to be one to watch.
keyPoints: The 2026 Rock and Roll Hall of Fame class includes Phil Collins, Oasis, Sade, Wu-Tang Clan, Iron Maiden, Billy Idol, Queen Latifah, Joy Division/New Order and Luther Vandross
Oasis's induction comes during their highly anticipated reunion tour after a 16-year split
Wu-Tang Clan's induction is a landmark moment for hip-hop's place in rock history
Luther Vandross will be honoured posthumously after his death in 2005
Acts are eligible for the Hall of Fame 25 years after their first commercial recording
articles.wikiSearchQuery: Rock and Roll Hall of Fame 2026 inductees

articles.title: Sony's CinemaCon 2026: Spider-Man, Zelda, and More
articles.summary: Sony dropped major news at CinemaCon 2026, showing trailers for Spider-Man: Brand New Day, Beyond the Spider-Verse, Resident Evil, and Jumanji, plus updates on the Legend of Zelda movie. CinemaCon is the annual Las Vegas convention where studios pitch their upcoming films to theater owners, making it one of the first places the public gets a real look at what's coming. The Sony panel was packed with franchise announcements, signaling a huge slate ahead for fans.
articles.content:
Sony brought the heat to CinemaCon 2026 on Tuesday, April 14, and it did not hold back. The studio gave theater owners and the world a look at some of its biggest upcoming films, and the crowd had a lot to react to. Two Spider-Man titles alone would have been enough to get people talking.

Spider-Man: Brand New Day and Beyond the Spider-Verse both got their moments in the spotlight. Brand New Day is the next live-action Spider-Man chapter, while Beyond the Spider-Verse is the long-awaited follow-up to the beloved animated Spider-Verse series. Fans have been waiting on Beyond the Spider-Verse for a while now since Across the Spider-Verse landed in 2023 and ended on a cliffhanger, so even a glimpse of new footage is a very big deal.

Sony also showed off footage from a new Resident Evil film and a new Jumanji movie. Resident Evil is one of the longest-running video game movie franchises around, and Jumanji has been a box office machine since its 2017 reboot with Dwayne Johnson and Kevin Hart. Both returning to the big screen means Sony is leaning hard into its proven properties.

On top of all that, Sony shared fresh news on the Legend of Zelda movie. The Zelda film has been one of the most talked-about video game adaptations in development, and any update on it is big news for gaming fans. Sony clearly wants people excited about going to the movies, and after this panel, it is hard not to be.
keyPoints: Sony showed trailers for Spider-Man: Brand New Day and Beyond the Spider-Verse at CinemaCon 2026
Beyond the Spider-Verse has been highly anticipated since Across the Spider-Verse's 2023 cliffhanger ending
New Resident Evil and Jumanji films were also previewed
Sony shared updates on the Legend of Zelda movie adaptation
CinemaCon is the major annual event where studios pitch upcoming films to theater owners

articles.title: OpenAI Buys Hiro to Put Finance Inside ChatGPT
articles.summary: OpenAI has acquired Hiro, an AI personal finance startup, in a move that signals the company is building financial planning directly into ChatGPT. The deal shows OpenAI's ambition to make ChatGPT a tool people use not just for creative tasks but for managing their actual money and life decisions. This is a major step toward ChatGPT becoming a personal assistant that handles things that really matter.
articles.content:
OpenAI just bought Hiro, an AI personal finance startup, and the message is pretty clear. ChatGPT is coming for your budgeting app. The acquisition, reported by TechCrunch on 14 April 2026, points directly to a new capability OpenAI is building into ChatGPT: financial planning. This is not a small side project. It is a statement about where OpenAI thinks ChatGPT needs to go next.

Hiro was built around the idea of making personal finance more accessible through AI. Think of it as a smart financial coach that lives on your phone, helping you understand your spending, plan for big goals, and make sense of your money without needing to hire an expensive human advisor. That kind of tool has obvious appeal for younger users who are often priced out of traditional financial advice but still need real guidance on saving, debt, and investing.

For OpenAI, adding Hiro's technology and team to ChatGPT is a way to deepen the daily relevance of the product. Right now ChatGPT is incredibly useful but still feels optional for most people. If it starts helping you track your budget, flag when you are overspending, or plan for a house deposit, it becomes something you open every single day out of necessity. That stickiness is enormously valuable for a company competing hard against Google, Anthropic, and others in the AI assistant space.

There are real questions here too. Personal finance data is among the most sensitive information anyone holds. If ChatGPT is going to offer financial planning, OpenAI will need to earn a level of user trust that goes well beyond what is required for writing an email or summarising a document. Regulators in the US and Europe are already watching AI companies closely, and moving into financial services will only increase that scrutiny.

Still, the direction of travel is obvious. OpenAI wants ChatGPT to be the one app you reach for when life gets complicated, whether that is drafting a cover letter, planning a trip, or figuring out why your bank balance looks nothing like your salary. Hiro is a key piece of that puzzle. Watch this space closely because the ChatGPT you use a year from now could look very different from the one you opened this morning.
keyPoints: OpenAI has acquired Hiro, an AI personal finance startup
The deal signals that OpenAI is building financial planning features into ChatGPT
Hiro was designed to make personal finance coaching more accessible through AI
The move would make ChatGPT more relevant to users on a daily basis
Handling sensitive financial data will raise new questions about trust and regulation for OpenAI

articles.title: Google, Microsoft and Meta Track You Even After You Opt Out
articles.summary: An independent audit has found that Google, Microsoft and Meta are all tracking users even after those users have explicitly opted out of tracking. The audit's findings are being described in stark terms, with one expert calling the opt-out system 'theatrical political posture' rather than real privacy protection. This is not a minor bug or a technicality. It is a fundamental challenge to the idea that privacy controls actually work.
articles.content:
You hit opt out and thought you were done. You were not. A new independent audit has found that Google, Microsoft and Meta are all continuing to track user data even after people have gone through the steps to opt out of tracking. The audit, reported by 404 Media, is one of the clearest demonstrations yet that the privacy controls these companies offer may be more about appearances than actual protection.

One of the experts quoted in the audit did not hold back. They described the point where tracking data flows as 'the Strait of Hormuz in the data economy', meaning it is a critical chokepoint. Their conclusion was blunt. 'If you want to make a change, this is where you cut it off. Anything short of that is theatrical political posture.' That quote alone should make you stop and think about every privacy toggle you have ever clicked.

This matters enormously right now because regulators in both the US and Europe have spent years building privacy frameworks around the assumption that opt-out systems are meaningful. The GDPR in Europe and various state-level privacy laws in the US all lean heavily on the idea that users can consent or refuse. If that foundation is hollow, then the entire regulatory structure built on top of it is also shaky.

Google, Microsoft and Meta are three of the most powerful data businesses on the planet. The fact that all three appear in the same audit with the same finding suggests this is not an accident or an oversight by one rogue team. It looks like a feature, not a bug, of how the current data economy is built. For anyone who cares about where their personal information goes, this audit is a wake-up call worth taking seriously.
keyPoints: An independent audit found Google, Microsoft and Meta all track users even after opt-out
One expert described current opt-out systems as 'theatrical political posture' rather than real privacy
The findings undermine the regulatory frameworks built around user consent in both the US and Europe
All three companies appearing in the same audit suggests this is a systemic industry issue
This is a major challenge to the idea that privacy settings on mainstream platforms actually work
articles.wikiSearchQuery: Internet privacy opt out tracking data

articles.title: 1,000 Creatives Say No to Paramount and Warner Bros. Merger
articles.summary: Over 1,000 Hollywood professionals have signed an open letter flatly opposing the $111 billion merger between Paramount Skydance and Warner Bros. Discovery. The group says the deal will wipe out jobs and crush creative opportunity across the industry. Big names including Joaquin Phoenix, Ben Stiller, and Denis Villeneuve are among those who put their names to it.
articles.content:
More than 1,000 Hollywood creatives have come together to sign an open letter calling out the proposed $111 billion merger between Paramount Skydance and Warner Bros. Discovery. The letter does not mince words. It says the deal is bad for workers, bad for storytellers, and bad for the culture of filmmaking itself.

The names attached to this letter carry serious weight. Joaquin Phoenix, Ben Stiller, and Denis Villeneuve are among the signatories. These are not people on the fringes of the industry. They are Oscar winners, blockbuster directors, and some of the most respected voices working in Hollywood today. When talent at that level speaks up together, the industry has to pay attention.

The core concern is consolidation. When two media giants this size merge, history shows that jobs go first. Creative teams get cut, projects get shelved, and the number of people with real power over what gets made shrinks dramatically. The letter argues that fewer power centers means fewer chances for original, diverse, and risky storytelling to survive.

The Paramount Skydance and Warner Bros. Discovery merger is still working its way through regulatory review. Deals of this scale need approval from government bodies, and public opposition from this many high-profile figures could add pressure to that process. Whether it slows the merger down or not, this letter makes clear that the people who actually make the movies and shows are not on board.
keyPoints: Over 1,000 Hollywood professionals signed a letter opposing the $111 billion Paramount Skydance and Warner Bros. Discovery merger
Signatories include Joaquin Phoenix, Ben Stiller, and Denis Villeneuve
The group says the merger will eliminate jobs and reduce creative opportunities
The deal is still under regulatory review
Mass talent opposition at this scale is rare and could influence the approval process
articles.wikiSearchQuery: Paramount Skydance Warner Bros Discovery merger

articles.title: Uber and Nuro Launch Lucid Robotaxi Tests in San Francisco
articles.summary: Uber and Nuro have started testing a premium robotaxi service in San Francisco, letting Uber employees hail a self-driving Lucid vehicle through the Uber app. This is a big deal because it brings together three major names in one vehicle and hints at where premium ride-hailing is heading. The Lucid robotaxi is electric, high-end, and fully autonomous, which is a very different experience from your average Uber Pool.
articles.content:
Uber and Nuro are testing something genuinely new in San Francisco right now. Uber employees can open the Uber app and hail a Lucid robotaxi as part of an early trial for a premium autonomous ride service. No driver. A luxury Lucid electric vehicle. Ordered through the app you already have on your phone.

Nuro is an autonomous vehicle company that has been working on self-driving delivery and passenger tech for years. Lucid is the electric vehicle maker known for its high-end Lucid Air sedan, which has one of the longest ranges of any electric car on the market. Pairing Nuro's autonomy software with a Lucid vehicle and putting it on Uber's platform is a smart combination of three companies that each bring something different to the table.

Right now the testing is limited to Uber employees in San Francisco, which is a common first step before a wider rollout. San Francisco has been the proving ground for robotaxi services from Waymo and others for a while now, so the city is already used to autonomous vehicles on its streets. Starting with employees means Uber and Nuro can collect real-world data and work out any issues before opening it up to the public.

The bigger picture here is that Uber is clearly not trying to own the autonomous vehicle technology itself. Instead Uber is positioning its app as the place where you access whatever the best autonomous vehicle option is, whether that is Waymo, Nuro, or someone else. If this Lucid robotaxi test goes well, it could become one of the fanciest ways to get across the city without a driver in sight.
keyPoints: Uber and Nuro are testing a premium robotaxi service in San Francisco using Lucid vehicles
Currently only available to Uber employees as an early trial
Nuro handles the autonomous driving technology while Lucid provides the electric vehicle
Uber is building its app as the hub for multiple autonomous vehicle partners rather than owning the tech itself
San Francisco is already a major testing ground for robotaxi services including Waymo

articles.title: Moya Brennan, Clannad's Soul, Dies at 73
articles.summary: Moya Brennan, lead singer and harpist of Irish folk group Clannad, has died aged 73. Known as the first lady of Celtic music, Brennan won both Grammy and Emmy awards and is widely credited with bringing Irish music and lyrics to a global audience. Her voice defined some of the most beloved Celtic recordings ever made.
articles.content:
Moya Brennan, the singer and harpist who led Clannad for decades, died on Tuesday 14 April 2026 at the age of 73. The news came from The Guardian, and the loss is already being felt deeply across the music world. Brennan was the kind of artist who did not just perform Irish folk music. She made the world fall in love with it.

Clannad formed in the early 1970s in Gweedore, County Donegal, Ireland, drawing from a family steeped in traditional Gaelic music. Moya Brennan was the eldest sibling and the unmistakable voice at the centre of the group. Clannad blended traditional Irish folk with ambient and new age sounds in a way nobody had quite heard before, and Brennan's ethereal vocals were the reason it worked so well.

Over her career, Brennan picked up both a Grammy Award and an Emmy Award, remarkable achievements for an artist rooted in a genre that mainstream industry often overlooked. The Grammy in particular recognised her solo work, which ran alongside her Clannad career and brought her even wider audiences. She was often called the first lady of Celtic music, and honestly, no other title fits better.

In her later years Brennan had been living with illness, according to The Guardian, but she remained a beloved figure in Irish cultural life. She leaves behind a catalogue that shaped how the world hears Ireland. From the haunting theme she sang for the TV series Robin of Sherwood to her own solo records, Moya Brennan's voice was a once in a generation thing. This one hurts.
keyPoints: Moya Brennan, lead singer of Clannad, died on 14 April 2026 aged 73
She won both a Grammy Award and an Emmy Award during her career
Brennan was widely called the first lady of Celtic music
Clannad helped bring Irish and Gaelic music to a global audience
She also had a successful solo career alongside her work with Clannad

articles.title: The Super Mario Galaxy Movie Is 2026's Biggest Film So Far
articles.summary: The Super Mario Galaxy Movie has become the highest-grossing film of 2026 so far, despite receiving poor reviews from critics. The film is a sequel to the 2023 The Super Mario Bros. Movie, which became one of the most successful video game adaptations in cinema history. Audiences are clearly still hungry for more Mario, even if critics are not convinced.
articles.content:
Critics are not feeling it. Audiences absolutely are. The Super Mario Galaxy Movie has just become the biggest film of 2026 so far at the box office, continuing one of the most remarkable franchise success stories in recent cinema history. The film is a sequel to The Super Mario Bros. Movie from 2023, which was a co-production between Universal Pictures and Nintendo and earned over 1.3 billion dollars worldwide, making it the highest-grossing video game film ever at the time.

The fact that The Super Mario Galaxy Movie is cleaning up despite poor reviews says a lot about the state of blockbuster cinema right now. Families and Nintendo fans are not waiting for a Rotten Tomatoes score before buying tickets. The Super Mario brand has a level of global recognition and emotional loyalty that very few properties can match. Mario is not just a game character. He is a childhood memory for multiple generations of people across the world.

The title is also a smart move. Super Mario Galaxy, the original 2007 Nintendo Wii game, is widely considered one of the greatest video games ever made. It has a devoted fanbase that was always going to show up for a film version. Tapping into that specific corner of Nintendo nostalgia gave The Super Mario Galaxy Movie a built-in audience before a single trailer dropped.

This is a story about the gap between critical consensus and audience appetite, and right now that gap is enormous. The Super Mario Galaxy Movie sitting at the top of the 2026 box office chart tells you everything about what is driving people back to cinemas. Big, colourful, familiar worlds with characters people love. Whatever the critics say, Nintendo and Universal have clearly found a formula that works.
keyPoints: The Super Mario Galaxy Movie is the highest-grossing film of 2026 so far
The film has achieved this despite receiving poor reviews from critics
It is a sequel to The Super Mario Bros. Movie from 2023 which earned over 1.3 billion dollars worldwide
The Super Mario Galaxy title connects to the beloved 2007 Nintendo Wii game
The film's success highlights a growing gap between critical opinion and audience demand
articles.wikiSearchQuery: The Super Mario Bros. Movie 2023 film

articles.title: LACMA's $724M David Geffen Galleries Finally Opens in LA
articles.summary: The Los Angeles County Museum of Art has unveiled the David Geffen Galleries, a stunning new building that cost $724 million and took a decade to get off the ground. Designed to float above Wilshire Boulevard, the structure is being called one of the most daring pieces of architecture in the United States right now. The New York Times described it as dazzling and pugnacious, and says it firmly plants Los Angeles back on the map as a city that loves to experiment with bold design.
articles.content:
After ten years of arguments, budget debates, and enormous public scrutiny, the David Geffen Galleries at LACMA are finally open. The building sits on Wilshire Boulevard in Los Angeles and it does something genuinely wild: it appears to float above the street. That is not a metaphor. The structure is engineered to hover over the road below, which is the kind of architectural ambition that either makes history or becomes a cautionary tale. Based on the early reviews, this one is leaning toward history.

The price tag is $724 million, which makes it one of the most expensive museum buildings ever constructed in the United States. That number has been controversial for years. Los Angeles is a city with enormous inequality, and spending that much on a cultural institution drew real criticism. But supporters have always argued that LACMA needed a landmark building to match its world-class collection, and that a great museum building pays dividends to a city for generations.

The design comes from Swiss architect Peter Zumthor, who is known for creating buildings that feel almost meditative. He is not a flashy architect in the Instagram sense, but his work tends to stop people in their tracks. The David Geffen Galleries building, named after the billionaire entertainment mogul David Geffen who donated significantly to the project, has been described by the New York Times as reasserting Los Angeles's role as a petri dish for experimental design. That is a big claim but the images back it up.

LACMA itself, which stands for the Los Angeles County Museum of Art, is the largest art museum in the western United States. It has been pieced together from several older buildings over the decades, and that patchwork quality always made the campus feel a little disjointed. The David Geffen Galleries are meant to unify the experience and give the whole institution a front door worthy of the collection inside.

Why does this matter beyond architecture fans? Because cities are partly defined by their great public buildings. The Guggenheim Bilbao transformed a Spanish city. The Centre Pompidou redefined Paris. Los Angeles has long been a cultural powerhouse in film, music, and art, but its museum landscape has lagged behind New York and European capitals. The David Geffen Galleries opening in April 2026 feels like a genuine shift. It is the kind of building that will end up on mood boards, in films, and in conversations about what American cities can still dare to build.
keyPoints: The David Geffen Galleries at LACMA have opened after a decade of planning and controversy
The building cost $724 million and is engineered to appear to float above Wilshire Boulevard
Swiss architect Peter Zumthor designed the structure, which is getting strong early reviews
The project is named after entertainment mogul David Geffen, who made a major donation
Critics say the building reasserts Los Angeles as a city willing to take real risks with design
articles.wikiSearchQuery: Los Angeles County Museum of Art

articles.title: Bremont Supernova Chronograph Is Going to the Moon
articles.summary: Bremont's Supernova Chronograph will be strapped to Astrolab's FLIP rover when it lands on the lunar surface later this year. This makes the Bremont Supernova Chronograph one of the very few watches ever sent to the moon, and the first attached to a rover chassis. It is a huge moment for the British watchmaker and a wild flex for anyone who loves watches and space in equal measure.
articles.content:
Bremont is sending its Supernova Chronograph to the moon. Not a replica. Not a render. The actual watch will be bolted onto the chassis of Astrolab's FLIP rover, which is scheduled to land on the lunar surface later this year. It is the kind of headline that sounds made up but is very real.

Astrolab is a California-based space robotics company and FLIP is its flagship lunar rover. FLIP stands for Field-Deployable Rover, and it is designed to carry payloads across the moon's surface. Bremont's Supernova Chronograph will ride along as a permanent fixture on the outside of the rover, exposed to the extreme temperatures and radiation of the lunar environment.

Bremont is a British luxury watch brand that has long leaned into its connections with aviation and exploration. Sending the Supernova Chronograph to the moon is the ultimate proof of concept for a watchmaker that builds its entire identity around watches that can survive brutal conditions. The moon's surface swings between around 127 degrees Celsius in sunlight and minus 173 degrees Celsius in darkness. That is about as brutal as it gets.

For watch fans this is a genuinely exciting moment. The Bremont Supernova Chronograph joins a tiny and legendary list of timepieces that have made it to the moon. If you have ever wanted a reason to care about Bremont, this is it.
keyPoints: Bremont's Supernova Chronograph will be physically attached to Astrolab's FLIP rover on the moon
The FLIP rover is built by California space robotics company Astrolab and is designed for lunar surface missions
The moon's surface hits extremes of plus 127 and minus 173 degrees Celsius, making this a serious durability test
This puts Bremont in a very small club of watchmakers with a timepiece on the moon

articles.title: Chess's Weirdest Cheating Mystery From 1993 Finally Solved
articles.summary: A group of gamblers who disguised a player and entered him in the 1993 World Open chess tournament in Philadelphia under the name of a mathematician who died in 1957 have finally been unmasked, more than three decades later. The bizarre scheme, which stumped the chess world for years, is one of the strangest cheating incidents in the history of competitive chess. The full story is being revealed now through new reporting that pulls back the curtain on who was really behind it.
articles.content:
In July 1993, someone walked into the World Open chess tournament in Philadelphia wearing a disguise and playing under a false name. Not just any false name either. The name belonged to a mathematician who had died back in 1957. For over 30 years, nobody knew who this mystery player actually was or who had sent them in.

The World Open is one of the biggest open chess tournaments in the United States, drawing hundreds of players and serious prize money. That made it an attractive target for gamblers looking to fix results. The scheme was elaborate: put a strong player in under a fake identity, let them perform above the level the tournament expected, and cash in on the bets placed on the side. It was bold, weird, and it worked long enough to disappear into legend.

Now, according to new reporting from Wired, the gamblers behind the scheme have finally been identified. The investigation pieced together who organized the plan and who the disguised player actually was. It is the kind of cold case resolution that feels almost cinematic, a mystery that lingered for 33 years before someone finally cracked it open.

Chess cheating stories have taken on a new life in recent years, especially after the 2022 controversy involving Magnus Carlsen and Hans Niemann that put cheating in competitive chess front and center in mainstream conversation. But this 1993 case predates all of that and is arguably stranger than anything that came after. It is a reminder that the chess world has always had a shadowy side that goes far beyond the board.

What makes this story so compelling is how much effort went into the cover-up and how long it held. Using the name of a dead person, showing up in disguise, playing in a major open tournament with hundreds of witnesses around, it should have unraveled fast. The fact that it did not says a lot about how hard identity was to verify before the internet age. This is a genuinely wild piece of sports history that deserves way more attention than it gets.
keyPoints: In July 1993, a disguised player entered the World Open chess tournament in Philadelphia using the name of a mathematician who died in 1957
The scheme was orchestrated by gamblers looking to profit from manipulated results
The identities of those behind the scheme remained unknown for over 30 years
New reporting from Wired has finally unmasked the gamblers involved
The story is resurfacing amid renewed public interest in chess cheating controversies

articles.title: Rolex Kills the Pepsi GMT-Master II and Cookie Monster
articles.summary: Rolex has officially pulled the iconic Pepsi GMT-Master II and the white gold Cookie Monster Submariner Date from its active catalogue. The discontinued models include the stainless steel ref. 126710BLRO and the white gold ref. 126659BLNR, two of the most recognisable and coveted watches Rolex has ever made. For collectors and watch fans, this is a very big deal.
articles.content:
Rolex just made it official. The Pepsi GMT-Master II and the white gold Cookie Monster Submariner Date are gone from the active Rolex catalogue. Both watches were confirmed discontinued as of today, 14 April 2026, and the watch world is already reacting.

If you are new to these names, here is a quick explainer. The Pepsi GMT-Master II gets its nickname from its red and blue bezel, which looks like the colours on a Pepsi can. The stainless steel version carries the reference number 126710BLRO. The Cookie Monster Submariner Date is a white gold Rolex Submariner with a vivid blue dial and bezel, and its reference number is 126659BLNR. Both watches earned cult status not just among collectors but in pop culture, appearing on wrists from boardrooms to music videos.

Discontinuing a reference is one of the most powerful moves Rolex makes. Once a model is officially gone, secondary market prices almost always rise. The Pepsi and Cookie Monster were already trading well above retail on the grey market before today. Now that they are confirmed dead, expect those prices to climb further and fast. Anyone who bought one at retail is sitting on a very healthy investment.

Rolex does not explain its decisions publicly, and it rarely comments on why a reference gets cut. But this sweeping discontinuation suggests the brand is clearing space, possibly for new references or updated designs. Whatever comes next, the Pepsi GMT-Master II ref. 126710BLRO and the Cookie Monster ref. 126659BLNR are now a closed chapter. And like most closed chapters in Rolex history, they are only going to become more valuable with time.
keyPoints: Rolex has officially discontinued the Pepsi GMT-Master II ref. 126710BLRO and the white gold Cookie Monster Submariner Date ref. 126659BLNR
Both watches were among the most recognisable and sought-after Rolex models ever made
Discontinued Rolex references almost always rise in value on the secondary market
Rolex has not given a public reason for the discontinuation
This may signal upcoming new references or updated designs from Rolex

articles.title: Sabrina Carpenter's Zaghrouta Reaction Sparks Global Meme Wave
articles.summary: Sabrina Carpenter made a confused face on stage after a fan performed a zaghrouta, a traditional Middle Eastern vocal celebration, and the internet ran with it instantly. The moment sparked both controversy and an apology from Carpenter, who many felt dismissed a beautiful cultural tradition. The resulting meme format has now spread across platforms, turning an awkward concert second into a full-blown cultural conversation.
articles.content:
So here is what happened. At a recent concert, a fan in the crowd performed a zaghrouta, which is a high-pitched, rapid trilling sound used as a joyful celebration in many Middle Eastern and North African cultures. It is the kind of cheer you hear at weddings, festivals, and big moments of communal joy. Sabrina Carpenter heard it from the stage and responded with a visibly confused expression, which was caught on camera and shared almost immediately.

That photo of Carpenter looking bewildered became a reaction image almost overnight. Know Your Meme documented the spread on April 13, 2026, noting how quickly the image was cropped, captioned, and remixed across social platforms. People used the image to express confusion about anything from work emails to complicated food orders. It is the kind of accidental internet gold that no PR team could plan.

But the moment was not all laughs. Many people, particularly those from communities where the zaghrouta is a deeply meaningful tradition, felt that Carpenter's reaction came across as dismissive or mocking of something sacred. The criticism spread fast online, and Carpenter issued an apology, acknowledging that she did not understand what she was hearing in the moment and that she regretted making the fan feel unwelcome.

The story has ended up in a genuinely interesting place. You have the apology, the memes, and a real conversation about how unfamiliar cultural expressions can get misread in real time on a live stage. Sabrina Carpenter has been one of pop's biggest names since her 2024 album Short n' Sweet exploded globally, so everything she does gets magnified. This one landed in messy, human, very online territory. And somehow that makes it more interesting than a clean PR moment ever would.
keyPoints: A fan performed a zaghrouta at a Sabrina Carpenter concert and Carpenter responded with visible confusion
A zaghrouta is a traditional joyful vocal cheer used across Middle Eastern and North African cultures
Carpenter's confused expression became a viral reaction image almost instantly
Many people felt her reaction was dismissive of a meaningful cultural tradition and she issued an apology
The moment sparked memes, criticism, and a wider conversation about cultural awareness at live events

articles.title: New LACMA Building Is Bold, Beautiful, and Worth the Hype
articles.summary: The Los Angeles County Museum of Art has a stunning new building that literally floats above Los Angeles, and critics are calling it daring and dazzling. LACMA is the largest art museum in the western United States, and its new structure is one of the most talked-about architecture projects in the country right now. The building marks a major new chapter for one of LA's most important cultural institutions.
articles.content:
The Los Angeles County Museum of Art has a new home, and it is genuinely something to see. The new LACMA building has been described as floating above Los Angeles, which sounds like marketing language until you actually look at it. The design is bold in a way that big civic buildings rarely manage to be.

LACMA has been working toward this moment for years. The museum has been one of the most visited art institutions in the United States for decades, and its old campus was a patchwork of buildings that never quite felt cohesive. This new structure was designed to change that entirely, giving LACMA a single unified home that matches the ambition of its collection.

The New York Times called the building daring and dazzling, which is high praise for a project that has faced its share of scrutiny over the years. Big public architecture projects in Los Angeles tend to attract debate, and LACMA's new building was no exception. Cost, design choices, and timelines were all discussed at length before the doors even opened. But the finished product seems to be winning people over.

For Los Angeles as a city, this is a genuinely big cultural moment. LA has been growing its reputation as one of the great art cities in the world, and having a landmark museum building to match that reputation matters. A lot of cities would love to have a cultural anchor this striking sitting right in the middle of them.

If you are anywhere near Los Angeles right now, this is worth putting on your list. New landmark buildings that actually live up to the hype do not come along very often. LACMA's new home sounds like one of the rare ones that earns every bit of the excitement around it.
keyPoints: LACMA's new building has opened in Los Angeles and is receiving glowing early reviews
The New York Times described it as daring and dazzling
The new structure replaces LACMA's old patchwork campus with a single unified building
LACMA is the largest art museum in the western United States
The building is seen as a major moment for Los Angeles as a global art city
articles.wikiSearchQuery: Los Angeles County Museum of Art new building
